agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"The product team is requesting an 'auto-split' feature that would automatically divide shipments exceeding 500 kg into multiple sub-shipments, each assigned to a different carrier, bypassing supervisor involvement. This directly conflicts with two foundational invariants in the existing production domain model.","bounded_context":"Shipment Management / Carrier Assignment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Shipment","definition":"A unit of goods to be transported, which must have exactly one assigned Carrier at all times from assignment through dispatch."},{"term":"Carrier","definition":"The entity responsible for physically transporting a Shipment. Assigned 1:1 to a Shipment."},{"term":"Carrier Assignment","definition":"The binding of exactly one Carrier to exactly one Shipment. Once locked, only a Supervisor can change it."},{"term":"Supervisor","definition":"A privileged role that is the only authority permitted to reassign a Carrier on a Shipment."},{"term":"Sub-Shipment","definition":"(Proposed, not yet in domain) A child Shipment created by splitting a parent Shipment. Does not exist in the current model."},{"term":"Auto-Split","definition":"(Proposed, not yet in domain) An automated process that decomposes a single Shipment into multiple Sub-Shipments based on a weight threshold."},{"term":"Dispatch","definition":"The point at which a Shipment leaves the facility. The invariant guarantees exactly one Carrier is assigned at this moment."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a state integrity constraint. The entire downstream process — tracking, liability, billing, proof of delivery — depends on a 1:1 Shipment-to-Carrier binding. If violated, the system cannot determine who is responsible for the goods.","testable_assertion":"For every Shipment in any state from 'assigned' through 'delivered', COUNT(assigned_carriers) == 1."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Once a Carrier is assigned to a Shipment, only a Supervisor can reassign.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is an authorization integrity constraint. Unrestricted reassignment creates liability gaps, audit trail breaks, and potential for fraud or operational chaos. The Supervisor gate ensures accountability.","testable_assertion":"Any Carrier reassignment event on a Shipment must have an associated Supervisor identity in its audit record."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"At the time of dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier assigned.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is the dispatch-time guarantee. Without it, goods leave the facility with ambiguous custody — a physical constraint violation (someone IS carrying it, the system must know who).","testable_assertion":"No Shipment can transition to 'dispatched' state unless exactly one Carrier is assigned."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A 500 kg shipment is split into two 250 kg sub-shipments, but one sub-shipment's carrier cancels after split. Who owns the orphaned sub-shipment? Does the parent shipment re-merge?","expected_behavior":"Undefined in current model. The parent Shipment concept does not exist. This creates a custody and liability gap."},{"scenario":"A shipment weighs exactly 500 kg — does it split or not? Off-by-one on the threshold.","expected_behavior":"Ambiguous. The spec says 'exceeds 500 kg' but boundary behavior must be explicitly defined."},{"scenario":"A shipment is auto-split after a carrier is already assigned. The original carrier assignment is effectively dissolved without supervisor approval.","expected_behavior":"This violates BR-2. The existing carrier's assignment is being revoked by an automated process, not a Supervisor."},{"scenario":"Customer queries tracking for their original shipment, which no longer exists as a single entity.","expected_behavior":"Undefined. The customer-facing Shipment identity has been fragmented. Tracking, notifications, and SLA measurement all break."},{"scenario":"Billing and liability: the original shipment had one commercial agreement. After split, costs may differ across multiple carriers.","expected_behavior":"Undefined. Rate cards, insurance, and liability allocation for sub-shipments are not addressed."},{"scenario":"A 1500 kg shipment splits into three sub-shipments. Two are delivered, one is lost. What is the claim and against whom?","expected_behavior":"Current model has no concept of partial delivery liability across multiple carriers for what was commercially a single shipment."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk. Splitting shipments across carriers fragments billing, insurance liability, and rate agreements. Without explicit commercial rules for sub-shipments, revenue leakage and dispute resolution costs are likely.","operational":"Severe. Introduces a parent-child shipment hierarchy that does not exist in the current model. Every downstream system — tracking, dispatch, proof of delivery, returns — assumes 1 Shipment = 1 Carrier. This is not a feature addition; it is a domain model restructuring.","user":"Customer-facing shipment tracking, notifications, and SLA commitments all assume a single shipment identity. Splitting fragments the customer experience and creates confusion about delivery status.","risk":"Critical. Two production invariants are directly violated. Shipping this as described would corrupt the integrity guarantees that the existing system relies on for custody tracking, audit compliance, and liability assignment.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Operations / Dispatch","impact":"Must handle a new parent-child shipment model. Dispatch workflows, warehouse pick/pack, and loading procedures all change."},{"group":"Carriers","impact":"Receive partial shipments they did not originally agree to. Contractual and SLA implications."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Their single shipment becomes multiple deliveries. Tracking, ETA, and support interactions all change."},{"group":"Finance / Billing","impact":"Must split invoicing, handle multi-carrier rate reconciliation, and manage partial delivery credits."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance","impact":"Chain of custody fragmentation. Liability for loss/damage becomes ambiguous across carriers."},{"group":"Supervisors","impact":"Their exclusive authority over carrier reassignment is bypassed by automation, undermining the control model."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Not a feature toggle — requires fundamental domain model evolution (Shipment aggregate restructuring, new Sub-Shipment entity, new lifecycle states)."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because the feature request as stated contains two direct contradictions with production invariants. The spec also has significant missing context: no definition of sub-shipment lifecycle, no commercial rules for split billing, no customer communication model, no liability framework. The request cannot be accepted as written.","high_confidence_areas":["The two invariant violations are unambiguous and clearly identified","The 1:1 Shipment-Carrier binding is a foundational domain constraint"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether the product team intends to relax the invariants or is unaware of them","What the commercial and legal framework for sub-shipments would be","Whether 500 kg is a regulatory, operational, or arbitrary threshold"],"assumptions":["The existing domain model as described is accurate and in production","No prior concept of sub-shipments or shipment hierarchies exists in the system"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split feature requires multiple carriers per shipment, directly violating BR-1 (exactly one Carrier per Shipment) and BR-3 (one Carrier at dispatch). These are production invariants — not policies. The feature as described is structurally incompatible with the current domain model.","affected_stakeholders":["Operations","Engineering","Legal","Customers"],"options":["A: Reject the feature as incompatible with current invariants.","B: Introduce a new 'ShipmentGroup' aggregate that contains multiple independent Sub-Shipments, each preserving the 1:1 Carrier invariant. The parent becomes a commercial/tracking wrapper, not a shipment itself.","C: Relax BR-1 to allow multi-carrier shipments — this is a fundamental domain model change requiring full impact analysis across all bounded contexts."],"recommendation":"Option B. Introduce a ShipmentGroup (or CompositeShipment) aggregate. Each Sub-Shipment is a first-class Shipment with its own Carrier assignment, preserving all existing invariants. The split operation creates new Shipments rather than violating the rules of an existing one. This requires significant design work but does not corrupt the domain model."},{"type":"contradiction","description":"The auto-split bypasses Supervisor authority for carrier reassignment, directly violating BR-2. The original Carrier's assignment is dissolved by an automated process without Supervisor approval.","affected_stakeholders":["Supervisors","Legal","Compliance"],"options":["A: Require Supervisor approval for every auto-split (defeats the 'auto' purpose).","B: Define auto-split as a distinct domain event that is NOT a reassignment — it is a shipment creation event. The original shipment is cancelled (with Supervisor pre-authorization via policy), and new shipments are created fresh.","C: Create a new permission tier (e.g., 'System' actor) that has reassignment authority for weight-based splits only."],"recommendation":"Option B. Model the split as shipment decomposition (cancel + create), not reassignment. This preserves BR-2's intent while enabling automation. Supervisor authority is maintained because they pre-authorize the split policy, not each individual split."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"The 500 kg threshold has no stated business justification. Is this a regulatory limit (vehicle weight laws), an operational constraint (single-carrier capacity), or an arbitrary product decision? The answer determines whether this is an invariant or a configurable policy.","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Operations","Legal"],"options":["Clarify with product team whether this is regulatory, operational, or preferential"],"recommendation":"Clarify before proceeding. If regulatory, it becomes an invariant with legal implications. If operational, it should be carrier-specific (different carriers have different capacity). If preferential, it should be configurable per customer or route."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Auto-split assigns multiple carriers to what is logically one Shipment","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: A Shipment must have exactly one assigned Carrier"},{"violation":"Auto-split dissolves an existing carrier assignment without Supervisor involvement","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Only a Supervisor can reassign a Carrier on a Shipment"},{"violation":"After split, the original Shipment either has zero or multiple Carriers at dispatch","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: At dispatch, every Shipment must have exactly one Carrier"}]}
